title: Expressing support for the designation of March 27, 2025, as "Tuskegee Airmen Commemoration Day", and calling on each State, the District of Columbia, and each territory to recognize the Tuskegee Airmen for their heroism, valor, and exemplary service to the Nation.
plain_summary: This bill wants to make March 27, 2025 a special day to honor the Tuskegee Airmen. The Tuskegee Airmen were brave Black pilots and other military workers who served during World War Two. Even though they faced unfair treatment because of their race, they served their country with great courage and skill. The bill asks all states and territories to recognize these heroes for their important service. Their brave actions helped end the practice of keeping Black and white soldiers separated in the military.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution supports making March twenty-seventh, two thousand twenty-five a special day called Tuskegee Airmen Commemoration Day. It also asks all states, Washington DC, and US territories to honor the Tuskegee Airmen for their brave and outstanding service to our country.

sections.title: Background Facts
sections.text: The Tuskegee Airmen were Black military pilots, flight crew members, ground support workers, and other support staff who served in the Army Air Corps from nineteen forty-one to nineteen forty-nine. Nine hundred ninety-two Tuskegee Airmen completed flight training at the Tuskegee Army Air Field in Tuskegee, Alabama. Four hundred fifty of these pilots were sent overseas to fight in combat. The Tuskegee Airmen faced terrible challenges including overcrowded classrooms, racist officers, and separate facilities because of segregation laws. During World War Two, the Tuskegee Airmen flew more than fifteen thousand combat missions and fought extensively in Europe and North Africa. Eighty-four Tuskegee Airmen died in battle, twelve died during training or non-combat missions, and thirty-two became prisoners of war when their planes were shot down. The Tuskegee Airmen earned seven hundred forty-four Air Medals, fourteen Bronze Stars, and eight Purple Hearts for their service. In nineteen forty-five, Tuskegee Airmen in the four hundred seventy-seventh Bombardment Group held a peaceful protest to end segregation at the officers club at Freeman Field, Indiana. Their peaceful protest methods later inspired civil rights protesters. The Tuskegee Airmen fought against fascism overseas and racism at home in what was called a Double Victory. Their efforts led President Truman to sign Executive Order nine thousand nine hundred eighty-one, which ended segregation in the military. Four Tuskegee Airmen named Benjamin O. Davis Jr., Daniel Chappie James, Lucius Theus, and Charles McGee later became Air Force generals. In two thousand six, Congress passed a law giving the Tuskegee Airmen the Congressional Gold Medal to recognize their unique military record that led to major changes in the Armed Forces. In two thousand eight, the Tuskegee Airmen National Historic Site opened at Moton Field in Tuskegee, Alabama to honor and teach people about the heroic actions of the Tuskegee Airmen. Very few Tuskegee Airmen are still alive today to share their stories of courage and bravery with younger Americans.

sections.title: Resolution
sections.text: The House of Representatives supports making March twenty-seventh, two thousand twenty-five Tuskegee Airmen Commemoration Day.
